title: Cat Eye Swollen Shut and Weeping: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's eye swollen and weeping? This guide explains the causes, high urgency level, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A swollen, weeping eye is a medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary care to prevent permanent vision loss or a ruptured globe. This condition can be caused by infections, ulcers, or injuries, and providing a photo helps the clinic prioritize your cat’s treatment.

What could be causing my cat's eye to swell and weep?

  • Corneal ulcers or scratches on the surface of the eye
  • Viral infections like Feline Herpesvirus
  • Bacterial conjunctivitis
  • Foreign objects, such as grass seeds, trapped behind the eyelid
  • Trauma resulting from a fight with another animal

Is a swollen and weeping eye a medical emergency for my cat?

  • Urgency Level: High. Any instance where a cat cannot open its eye should be treated as an emergency.
  • Eye conditions can worsen rapidly, potentially leading to permanent blindness within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Contact an emergency vet immediately if you notice yellow/green discharge, cloudiness, or frequent pawing at the eye.

How does providing a photo help with my cat's veterinary triage?

  • Helps the veterinary team determine the specific type of discharge and inflammation severity.
  • Identifies visible trauma to the cornea or foreign objects.
  • Allows the clinic to prioritize your cat's appointment and prepare diagnostic tools in advance.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ophthalmic emergencies in cats require rapid diagnosis and aggressive therapy to maintain vision. Swelling of the eyelids may be associated with systemic diseases such as Demodex cati, Notoedres cati (scabies), ringworm, and immune-mediated skin diseases. Common infectious pathogens, such as feline herpesvirus-1, Chlamydia, and Mycoplasma, frequently present as acute and recurrent conjunctivitis. Traumatic proptosis can occur following blunt trauma, where the globe is luxated from the orbit, and eyelid spasms prevent retraction, leading to corneal drying. Penetrating intraocular injuries can also cause intraocular hemorrhage.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1696)

faqs.question: Can I wait until tomorrow to see a vet?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to wait. Eye conditions are intensely painful and can lead to permanent vision loss very quickly. Immediate professional assessment is the safest course of action.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No. Many human medications contain ingredients that are toxic to cats or can cause a corneal ulcer to worsen. Never apply any medication to a cat's eye unless specifically directed by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: How can I comfort my cat until we reach the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a dim, quiet room to reduce light sensitivity. If you have an Elizabethan collar (cone), put it on immediately to prevent your cat from pawing at and further damaging the eye.
